title: Curry goat
subtitle: Jamaican bone-in goat or mutton in a rich curry gravy
excerpt: Bone-in goat or mutton pieces braised low and slow in Jamaican curry powder with potato, scotch bonnet, and a dark, fragrant gravy. A weekend and celebration dish across the Jamaican diaspora.
sourceNotes: Curry goat is one of the most iconic dishes of Jamaican cooking, introduced via the East Indian indentured workers who came to Jamaica in the nineteenth century and adapted using local ingredients including scotch bonnet and native spice profiles. It is distinct from South Asian goat curry in its use of Jamaican curry powder, which is typically milder and more fragrant than Madras-style blends. This version uses bone-in lamb neck fillet as a widely available substitute for goat; bone-in goat shoulder or mutton is the traditional choice. Note: a dedicated goat-meat ingredient should be added to the master ingredient table.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes very well. Cool completely and freeze in portions for up to 3 months. Reheat gently on the hob, adding a splash of water if the gravy has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a large pot. The flavour improves the next day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before. Rest overnight in the fridge and reheat gently before serving.

content.text: Curry goat takes time. The meat needs two hours of low, slow cooking before it gives up its grip on the bone and the gravy turns dark and fragrant with curry spice, scotch bonnet, and thyme. It is weekend food across the Jamaican diaspora, present at every Saturday party, christening, and reunion. The potato goes in halfway through, absorbing the curry gravy and softening to the point where it just holds together.

Use bone-in meat. The bones contribute gelatin to the gravy and keep the meat moist through the long cook. Boneless shoulder works but the gravy will be thinner. Marinate the meat in curry powder and salt for at least 30 minutes before browning — this helps the spice bond to the surface rather than washing off into the gravy.

items.prepNote: bone-in, cut into 5 cm pieces — or bone-in goat shoulder if available
Jamaican-style if available; otherwise standard mild curry powder
whole, or finely chopped for more heat

content.text: Toss the {{lamb-neck}} of lamb neck or goat pieces with {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, {{allspice}} of allspice, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Leave to marinate for at least 30 minutes at room temperature, or up to 24 hours in the fridge.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a heavy casserole or Dutch oven over medium-high heat until shimmering. Brown the meat pieces in batches, turning to colour on all sides — about 4 minutes per batch. Don't crowd the pan; the meat must fry, not steam. Remove to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} onions to the pot and cook for 8 minutes, stirring until softened and starting to colour. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{ginger-root}} of ginger and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{tomato}} tomatoes, {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{thyme-fresh}} of thyme sprigs, and the whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Stir well, scraping up any curry residue from the base of the pot.

Return the browned meat to the pot. Pour over enough water to just cover — about 600 ml. Bring to a simmer, then cover and cook over the lowest possible heat for 1 hour. Add the {{potato}} of potato, stir gently, and continue cooking for another 45–60 minutes until the meat is falling off the bone and the potato is cooked through. If the gravy is thin, remove the lid for the last 15 minutes to reduce it. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme sprigs before serving.

items.cause: Collagen not yet broken down; heat may be too low
items.fix: Continue cooking for another 30 minutes. Increase the heat slightly to maintain a gentle but consistent simmer. Bone-in pieces take longer than boneless

items.cause: Curry powder not sufficiently cooked into the aromatics before the liquid went in
items.fix: Cook the spice in the fat with the onions for a full minute before adding liquid — this toasts out the raw note. In a cooked curry, a longer simmer will smooth the flavour

items.symptom: Potato has dissolved into the gravy
items.cause: Added too early or heat too vigorous
items.fix: Add potato in the final 45–50 minutes only. Use waxy varieties rather than floury, which break down faster
Keep the chilli whole and handle carefully when removing. If too hot, stir in a tablespoon of plain yoghurt or coconut cream off the heat

content.text: Curry goat was brought to Jamaica by East Indian indentured workers after emancipation in the 1830s and 1840s. The spice tradition blended with local ingredients (scotch bonnet, allspice, local thyme) to produce something distinct from any South Asian curry. It became a cornerstone of Jamaican cooking, the Saturday party dish, the feast-day staple. In British Jamaican communities, it is the dish that most consistently appears at celebrations. Serve with rice and peas and a small pile of fried plantain.
